description.text:
For as long as humans have told stories, we’ve imagined creatures that transcend the natural world. Fantastical beings combining the features of animals, humans, and even plants appear across cultures, emerging in the most ancient myths and enduring in contemporary epics. The widespread presence of these supernatural beings, possessing the power to transform and be transformed, reflects a global impulse to make sense of both known and unknown worlds. Visual artists have given form to these imaginary creatures, resulting in some of the most fearsome, beloved, and extraordinary works of art ever made.

Set in the evocative atmosphere of The Met Cloisters, Creatures of Myth and Imagination: Europe and the Americas sheds light on a selection of works created on either side of the Atlantic Ocean between 500 and 1500 CE. The exhibition’s exploration of hybrid creatures deepens our understanding of their apparent necessity among diverse peoples. In the Americas, a complex gold pendant by a Tairona artist of northern Colombia, depicting a confrontational figure with hands on hips, a crocodile-like head, and an enormous headdress, would have reflected and expressed the wearer’s status and power. In Europe, ferocious dragons such as the one depicted on a monumental fresco from the monastery of San Pedro de Arlanza, Spain, took center stage to convey a multiplicity of meanings both sacred and profane.

With these and over 50 other objects, including sculpture, ceramics, ivories, textiles, paintings, and metalwork, Creatures of Myth and Imagination looks beyond a specific story, time, and place to explore larger questions about who we are and what connects us.
  
Exhibition Open: Through October 18, 2026

long_name.text: Creatures of Myth and Imagination: Europe and the Americas

long_name.text: Creatures of Myth and Imagination: Europe and the Americas

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This chapel, once part of a mastaba tomb, dominates the entrance to the Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art. In about 2350 2400 B.C., the monument was built for an Old Kingdom court official named Perneb at Saqqara, the great cemetery of Egypt's ancient capital Memphis. The Met was able to purchase this tomb chapel from the Egyptian government in 1913. A granite lion and a statue of another official also introduce the visitor to the monumentality and permanency of ancient Egypt's art and architecture.

long_name.text: The Entrance to the Art of Ancient Egypt

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The objects in this room record the early history of people in the Nile Valley beginning around 300,000 B.C. through the time of Egypt’s first pharaohs, whose last king died about 2700 B.C. Most pieces were excavated from burials and were items that people believed necessary for the next life. The images seen in and on many of these objects reflect life in and around the Nile River, floodplain, and the desert and many of the themes expressed continued into the Pharaonic period. One case presents sets of objects whose arrangements suggests how Predynastic burials of different periods looked.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The scenes of nature and of activities that surrounded the preparation for an individual’s afterlife are depicted on the walls of this tomb chapel. The chapel was created in about 2400 B.C. for a man called Neferiretenes, who was a judge and inspector of scribes. His relatives would have presented offerings in front of the stylized reproduction of an Egyptian doorway (false door) that symbolized the connection between this world and the next. Later the tomb was taken over for Raemkai, a king’s son, whose name has replaced that of Neferiretenes on the false door.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The Old Kingdom, which includes Dynasty 3‑6 and dates between 2700 and 2200 B.C., was a formative period in the development of monumental architecture and gave voice to many principles of Egyptian art. Although the pyramids are the most famous monuments from this time, decorated tomb chapels and statues of wealthy officials display the extraordinary talents of the sculptors who worked in stone and wood. In addition, exceptionally skillful artisans created other objects for the temples and tombs, such as stone vessels in Egyptian alabaster. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The imposing statue of Nebhepetre Mentuhotep in his festival cloak dominates the room. This ruler, whose family came from the southern area of Thebes (modern day Luxor), unified Egypt around 2050 B.C. after a period when the state fragmented into regional leadership. With the country’s reunification, Nebhepetre Mentuhotep’s rule guided a period of cultural revival, the vitality of which may be observed in the delicately carved reliefs largely from his funerary temple and the tomb of his wife, Queen Neferu, both structures located at Deir el-Bahri.

long_name.text: The Beginning of the Middle Kingdom

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Some of the most famous objects in The Met’s collection of Egyptian art are displayed in this gallery. The pristinely preserved models depicting food production, a garden, a graceful female offering bearer, and six magnificent boats were created to activate rituals that would provide the chief royal steward, Meketre, with the activities and goods they represented in the afterlife. Nearby is the funerary equipment of Wah, Meketre’s estate manager. His jewelry is the finest known from this time and his mummy mask portrays a vibrant face.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery displays Middle Kingdom objects from about 2000 to 1650 B.C. excavated by The Met in Thebes, organized as if in a storeroom. This arrangement allows visitors to study the range of objects that archaeologists find during excavation. The gallery also holds two cases of objects that once belonged to private collectors. The difference in the nature between these two groups is worth considering. Excavated objects come from a known place but their significance is often only understandable after much scholarly work, while pieces from private collections are chosen for their appeal and therefore often are in better condition.

long_name.text: The Early Middle Kingdom Study Room

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Tomb reliefs belonging to the treasurer Khety and the vizier Dagi were made during a period of cultural revival around 2000 B.C. Nearby the private papers of Heqanakht, a landowner whose family lived around 1950 B.C. may be viewed. These letters document that this ancient Egyptian landowner bullied and badgered his family and staff to take care of his affairs while he was away. In 1921, Met archaeologist Herbert E. Winlock excavated these four-thousand-year-old papyri that give us a glimpse into social and economic concerns of an ancient Egyptian family. [Translations of the letters are available on The Met’s website.]

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery displays important finds from the Museum's excavation of the pyramid complex of Amenemhat I, who reigned for some thirty-one years beginning around 1980 B.C. He was the founder of Dynasty 12, a cultural high point in the history of ancient Egypt. The room features reliefs from the king’s temples, an imposing offering table, a royal head from a sphinx, as well as objects once buried under the walls to protect his new temple.

long_name.text: From the Pyramid Complex of King Amenemhat I at Lisht North

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The objects in this gallery are exhibited as if in a storeroom, allowing visitors to discover their own favorite pieces among the thousands of relief fragments, figurines, scarabs, amulets, vessels, and pieces of personal jewelry dating between 1980 and 1550 B.C. Archaeologists, mostly working for The Met, excavated these pieces from Lisht in northern Egypt, as well as Abydos and Thebes in the southern part of the country in the early 20th century.

Twenty-one pairs of double columns with carved capitals border this cloister overlooking Fort Tryon Park and the Hudson River. The slender proportions of the stonework are typical of the late thirteenth century, while the almost abstract quality of the delicate, plant-inspired capitals betrays their Cistercian origin, from a monastery in Bonnefont-en-Comminges, not far from Toulouse.

Plants known to have grown in medieval Europe thrive in the nineteen raised beds in the Bonnefont Cloister. These plants are labeled and grouped to demonstrate their medieval uses such as medicine, food, and material for artists.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The pyramid complex of Lisht South once had a magnificent enclosure wall whose remains were later excavated by The Met’s archaeologists. Four of the massive relief panels that decorated this wall have been reconstructed for display in this gallery. Senwosret I, the king who built this complex, reigned for about forty-two years beginning around 1960 B.C. A headless statue of this king in a smooth dark stone dominates the gallery’s center. Other royal reliefs hang on the walls, while additional statues and tombstones (stelae) belonging to officials, from Senwosret I’s court as well as from other sites in Egypt, are presented.

long_name.text: From the Pyramid Temple of King Senwosret I at Lisht South

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery houses some of the finest examples of the creativity and skill available to Middle Kingdom and later rulers from about 2000 to 1550 B.C. Highlighted at the gallery’s entrance, the extraordinary jewelry of Princess Sithathoryunet dazzles visitors. Important pieces of royal sculpture grace the room, including the imposing sphinx of King Senwosret III and the guardian figure wearing a Red Crown that dates to about 1890 B.C. The Met’s “mascot,” the faience hippo known as William also resides here along with the faces of many officials. Jewelry from the time of the Hyksos speaks to the wealth commanded by these Egyptian kings bearing Semitic names, who ruled northern Egypt from a Delta stronghold known as Tell el Da’ba from about 1650 to 1550 B.C.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery features burial equipment of the elite officials and family members who resided at a provincial site called Meir, about 200 mi. south of Cairo. Their mummified bodies were often provided with masks, then placed inside human-shaped coffins that were deposited inside large, heavy and beautifully decorated rectangular coffins. The dead also received a variety of magically potent objects to protect them, some of which connected the dead person to Osiris, the god of the underworld. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

long_name.text: Burials from the Site of Meir

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Objects from The Met’s excavations introduce visitors to life in Egypt between 2000 and 1550 B.C. House models, furniture, and vessels are brought together alongside small figures of household deities and magical instruments. Other objects represent local industries, including agriculture, fishing, animal husbandry, and textile manufacturing, material from workshops, and objects indicative of trade. Four female tomb groups demonstrate how social status was reflected in burial practices and that of Senebtisi illustrates funerary equipment belonging to a member of the royal court in about 1800 B.C. Her apron is a rare piece of ritual jewelry that connects her to Osiris, the god of the underworld.

long_name.text: Life and Death in Middle Kingdom Egypt

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery emphasizes the excavations The Met conducted at Asasif, a cemetery east of Hatshepsut’s funerary temple at Deir el Bahri, near modern day Luxor. The display also includes objects from the site that The Met purchased from Lord Carnarvon, who sponsored excavations there with Howard Carter as his archaeologist. These fascinating tombs, dating around 1550 B.C., illustrate what was important funerary equipment at the time: coffins, vessels, toilet equipment, and jewelry. Their forms indicate the development of new styles that surfaced after the struggles of the Second Intermediate Period concluded about 1550 B.C. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

long_name.text: Early New Kingdom Tombs from the Asasif

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The greatest female king, Hatshepsut, commissioned the monumental sculptures in this gallery to adorn her spectacular funerary temple at Deir el-Bahri, near modern day Luxor. Sometime after her death around 1450 B.C., her co-regent and successor Thutmose III, who ruled more than fifty-four years, ordered the destruction of her images. In order to remove any memory of her rule, Hatshepsut’s sculptures were smashed into thousands of pieces. The Met excavated the fragments and painstakingly reassembled many destroyed sculptures, the most complete of which remained in Egypt. Twelve reassembled statues are exhibited here, re-creating the splendor of temple statuary destroyed some thirty-five hundred years ago.

long_name.text: Statues of the Female King Hatshepsut

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

On the east side, the cases contain objects deposited under the walls of Pharaoh Hatshepsut’s temple, including a large number of beautifully carved scarabs. In the center, the contents of Hatnefer’s burial are exhibited. Her son Senenmut gave her a worthy burial that included food, cosmetics, jewelry, furniture, linen sheets, and a gilded mask. Nearby, the funerary equipment of a Theban family has been bought together. A charming statue names and depicts each family member, including the father, Neferkhawet, who was in charge of an ancient workshop. This sculpture gives faces to the people who once used the adjacent burial goods.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

In this gallery, the objects are exhibited as if in a storeroom, allowing visitors to discover their own favorite pieces among the thousands of architectural fragments, small statues, tombstones (stelae), cosmetic containers, jewelry, and dozens of amulets dating between 1550 and 1300 B.C. Also of interest is the collection of cattle, goat, and sheep skulls with horns (bucrania) found as part of Pan Grave tombs, which date to about 1650 B.C. These pieces represent people whose heritage was most likely to be found among the nomadic people of Egypt’s Eastern desert.

long_name.text: The Early New Kingdom Study Room

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This rich equipment belonged to the three foreign wives of King Thutmose III, who reigned for fifty-four years beginning around 1480 B.C. The women had non-Egyptian Semitic names, suggesting they may have been daughters of rulers from neighboring countries married to the Egyptian pharaoh to strengthen political ties. Their burials, however, were outfitted almost entirely according to Egyptian customs and beliefs so that only their names indicate their foreign origin. The northern case contains golden funerary jewelry, while the southern case holds jewelry worn while participating in cultic activities during their lifetimes.

long_name.text: The Burials of Foreign Wives of Thutmose III

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

A number of unique works may be viewed in this gallery that date around 1400 B.C., and belong to a time when Egypt was at its peak in wealth and political stability, and the arts. Highlights include a battle relief representing Asiatics being trampled under the pharaoh's chariot, faces of King Amenhotep III, an exquisite sphinx in blue faience, the decorated side of Thutmose IV’s throne, and three delicately carved animals in ivory. Another case explores the participation of women in religious life.

Elements from the cloister of the Carmelite convent of Trie-en-Bigorre, near Toulouse, were dispersed after the French Revolution. Today some of these elements, along with pieces from nearby monuments, constitute this intimate space.

Biblical scenes, royal symbols, and aristocratic coats-of-arms adorn the double capitals. A central fountain, composed of late medieval and modern elements, stands in the center of a garden inspired by medieval images of meadows and woodlands. Small, trefoiled openings pierce the surrounding masonry walls, framing views of the adjacent Bonnefont Cloister and the wooded Fort Tryon Park below.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Malqata is the modern name of the festival-city built by King Amenhotep III around 1350 B.C. to celebrate his three jubilees. The city had two mudbrick palaces, a vast harbor, a temple, a viewing platform, administrative quarters, manufacturing areas, and small settlements. The objects on view include pieces of decorated floors and walls and a collection of richly painted pottery vessels. Fragments from glass vessels and amulets document the activities carried out within the city. Hundreds of ink inscriptions, which labelled the contents of containers used in the celebrations and smashed afterwards, are the source of the sherds on display.

long_name.text: Objects from Malqata, the Festival-City of Amenhotep III

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Works in this gallery mostly originate from Amarna, a site in Middle Egypt where Akhenaten, initially crowned Amenhotep IV, established his residence. Akhenaten, who reigned for seventeen years beginning around 1370 B.C., spread the exclusive worship of one aspect of the solar deity, the light of the sun, along with the veneration of himself and his queen, Nefertiti. In the arts, the king's pioneering religious ideas opened up an equally innovative period of naturalism and emotional expression. The gallery also displays art from the post-Amarna period, including several sculptures bearing the features of Tutankhamun.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The objects in this gallery are exhibited as if in a storeroom, allowing visitors to discover their own favorite pieces dating between about 1350 and 1100 B.C. The fragments along the north wall come from Amarna, King Akhenaten’s city, and belong to smashed statues of the royal family. These pieces continue to reveal information about the statuary program in the Amarna temples. On the south side are the remains from Tutankhamun's funeral activity discovered in 1907. Its find spot later helped to locate the king’s tomb in 1922. Late New Kingdom pieces on the east and west walls present material belonging to Ramesside culture and history.

long_name.text: The Late New Kingdom Study Room

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

In this gallery, the visitor once again has an opportunity to experience the monumental in Egyptian art. The granite jamb of a massive gateway probably comes from the funerary temple of Ramesses II, who reigned for an astonishing sixty-six years beginning around 1290 B.C. The limestone relief belongs, at about 575 B.C., to another gateway that was part of the palace of King Apries at Memphis. Stone coffins, statues, and royal heads are highlighted here as well, including an especially elegant gilded bronze statue of Ptah, the patron god of Memphis.

long_name.text: Monumental and Temple Art from around 1200-600 B.C.

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Contacts between Egyptians and non-Egyptians, interactions with divine beings, the rule of kings, and funerary activities are topics explored in this gallery through art dating from about 1300 to 1100 B.C., that is, during the time of the Ramesside kings. There is the spectacular funerary statue of the couple Yuny and Renenutet. Nearby objects display elements that speak to interactions with worlds beyond Egypt’s borders and the massive head of a captive stands out. Across the gallery, dynamic representations of two kings in vastly different materials illustrates the skill of royal workshops. Sketches on bits of limestone reveal the humanness of the ancient Egyptians.

long_name.text: The Art of the Ramesside Period

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Most Egyptian art that survives from about 1100 to 700 B.C. comes from workshops that produced beautiful objects dedicated to divine beings for temples. Much of the art displayed in this gallery is therefore identified as temple donations or cultic equipment. Many pieces reflect the high quality of metalworking associated with the time. Later when the Kushite kings conquered Egypt around 700 B.C., they continued to support artistic production, but added their own vigor to traditional Egyptian forms. At the gallery’s entrance, temple offerings in faience express elements of a well-known Egyptian story, “The Myth of the Faraway Goddess.”

long_name.text: The Third Intermediate Period and Kushite Art

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery presents an outstanding collection of painted coffins mostly excavated by The Met, dating from about 1300 to 700 B.C. The display clearly shows how these caskets could come in sets: outer coffin, inner coffin, and a lid laid over the mummified body. Many coffins are yellow in color and vividly decorated with detailed religious scenes of unparalleled variety. Not to be missed are the beautifully inscribed funerary papyri, especially the one belonging to Nany. These documents also come from the tombs along with boxes filled with funerary figurines known as shabtis. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

During Dynasty 26, beginning around 664 B.C., artists increasingly reflected on works made in earlier times, combining various elements into new creations. These objects exhibit sensitive modeling and brilliant surface treatments, especially in hard stones. The gallery has many pieces of royal sculpture as well as objects that once graced a temple or shrine, including two exquisite statuettes of deities. Nespekashuty’s unfinished tomb wall shows how work progressed while carving relief in stone. To the west, a series of shelves exhibit a variety of amulets and small sculptures that display the remarkable talents of artisans of the time.

long_name.text: The Art of the Late Period

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The reliefs in this gallery come from some of the temples commissioned by King Nectanebo I, whose rule began in 380 B.C., and Nectanebo II, who followed shortly thereafter. These were the last indigenous pharaohs of ancient Egypt and they invigorated the arts by carrying out extensive building programs. Statues from this time exhibit a remarkable sensitivity in the rendering of the human body, for example, the kneeling ritual figure delicately captures in wood a king in the action of honoring a divine being. The large stone slab known as a stela is the finest example of a type of magically potent monument and the ancient Egyptians believed that water poured over such an object had healing powers.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The reliefs exhibited in this space come from a chapel that King Seti I, who reigned around 1300 B.C., built at Abydos to honor his father, Ramesses I. The center scene shows the two kings worshiping the "fetish" of Osiris, a symbol of the chief god of the underworld and main deity worshipped at the pilgrimage site of Abydos. Another relief shows Ramesses' I face with signs of age: a rare example of realism in the representation of a royal individual.

long_name.text: Reliefs from a Temple of Ramesses I

description.text:
Works of art in precious materials on view in this gallery and in Gallery 014 convey the luxury and artistic sophistication of medieval artists and patrons.

Finely painted manuscripts range from works made for a Spanish monastery to a prayer book for French queens and princes. Ivory carvings include book covers carved in the time of Charlemagne, a box made at the court of the Muslim caliph at Cordoba, and a mirror back made for Parisian nobility. Goldsmiths' work includes enamels from Limoges and elsewhere, and jewelry from the eleventh through fifteenth century. From a princely collection comes a costly and rare enameled beaker that humorously depicts mischievous apes as they rob a sleeping peddler of his money and his clothes.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The objects in this gallery are exhibited as if in a storeroom, allowing visitors to discover their own favorite pieces. The range of objects displayed here will delight the viewer, who can study painted textiles, bronze statuettes representing a variety of deities, faience funerary figurines, and hundreds of amulets from the period between about 1100 B.C. and A.D. 300. In the center, several cases contain elaborately decorated coffins that are important to the understanding of ancient Egyptian funerary practices. The east wall case displays objects from the Nubian kingdom of Meroe. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Although small compared to most Egyptian temples, the Temple of Dendur is a graceful example of a typical pharaonic temple and is a highlight of any visit to The Met. Caesar Augustus built this structure by 10 B.C. in Lower Nubia, the region adjacent to ancient Egypt’s southern border, to connect the population living there to the Roman Empire. Therefore, the monument is dedicated not only to the goddess Isis and other Egyptian deities, but also to several indigenous Nubian gods and two local saints. In 1965, Egypt gave the Temple of Dendur to the U.S. in recognition of the support our government and its citizens gave Egypt towards saving their Nubian monuments.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery is devoted to departmental rotations. The sarcophagus that anchors the gallery space once belonged to a man named Wennefer, who lived in the fourth century B.C.

long_name.text: Temporary Exhibition Space for Departmental Rotations

description.text: This gallery focuses on the equipment needed for a good burial during the time of the Ptolemaic rulers, that is, from about 332 to 30 B.C. The mummified bodies of Irtirutja, Nesmin, and Tasheriteniset are still in their original wrappings and feature elaborate cartonnage decoration as well as gilded masks. A visitor should take note of the dynamic statuette of the jackal-headed god Anubis with his upraised arms. A papyrus scroll over 71 ft. long and inscribed with text and images from a set of spells known as the “Book of the Dead,” hangs on the wall opposite the coffins. Its many illustrations are fascinating to study. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

long_name.text: Funerary Equipment of the Ptolemaic Period

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Delicate faience inlays that once decorated a shrine, deity figurines in faience and bronze, and vessels of silver and bronze, all dating to the Ptolemaic era from 332 B.C. to 30 B.C., are among the objects displayed in this gallery. The group of limestone plaques with animal and human figures in relief represent significant donations to temple cults. There are also wonderful depictions of various royal figures, including those of famous queens: Berenike II, Arsinoe II, and Cleopatra VI, as well as a number of Ptolemaic kings. The wall case provides the visitor with additional examples of Ptolemaic art.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Artists, who were part of the Museum’s Graphic Expeditions to Egypt in the 1920s and 1930s, made the precise color copies (facsimiles) of scenes seen in this gallery that were then visible on the walls of New Kingdom tombs largely dating from about 1400 B.C. to 1100 B.C. They copied only what they saw, making the paintings to scale with exact coloring. These facsimiles are significant archival records of these tombs and Egyptologists continue to use them as important sources of information about this ancient culture.

long_name.text: Facsimiles of Ancient Egyptian Tomb Paintings

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Small exhibitions are regularly held in this space that focus on specific topics drawn from the culture and history of ancient Egypt. They highlight groups of objects from The Met’s collection of Egyptian art.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The wall cases display art from the early Roman Period (dating from 30 B.C. to A.D. 200), including a faience head of Augustus, the king who defeated Marc Antony at Actium, and superb panel portraits. During this period, people continued the custom of mummification, but Hellenistic and Roman traditions were also integrated into the funerary art. Consequently, panel portraits painted on wood using pigmented wax could replace the traditional idealized masks as may be seen in the case of the mummy of a man from Hawara, whose identity is unknown. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

long_name.text: Egypt under Roman Rule in the First Century AD

description.text:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The pieces in this gallery mark the last phase of ancient Egyptian culture as the last Egyptian temple closed around A.D. 400. The beautifully adorned mummy of a woman named Artemidora is the focus of the room. There are panel portraits, including the young boy, Eutyches, as well as funerary masks of plaster. One case holds glass vessels, while another has a fine example of funerary portrait of a woman painted on linen. Many objects here illustrate a mixture of Greco-Roman and Egyptian styles, for example the Isis-Aphrodite statue and the gold jewelry. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

long_name.text: Egypt under Roman Rule in the Second to Fourth Centuries AD

description.text:
Works of art in precious materials on view in this gallery and in Gallery 013 convey the luxury and artistic sophistication of medieval artists and patrons.

Finely painted manuscripts range from works made for a Spanish monastery to a prayer book for French queens and princes. Ivory carvings include book covers carved in the time of Charlemagne, a box made at the court of the Muslim caliph at Cordoba, and a mirror back made for Parisian nobility. Goldsmiths' work includes enamels from Limoges and elsewhere, and jewelry from the eleventh through fifteenth century. From a princely collection comes a costly and rare enameled beaker that humorously depicts mischievous apes as they rob a sleeping peddler of his money and his clothes.

The Leonard N. Stern Collection on Loan from the Hellenic RepublicThe display of the Leonard N. Stern Collection of Cycladic Art at The Met is the result of a historic 50-year partnership among The Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Ministry of Culture of the Hellenic Republic, and the Museum of Cycladic Art in Athens—approved by Greek parliament in 2022. The partnership brings 161 splendid Cycladic works of art acquired by the businessman and philanthropist Leonard Stern over some 40 years to The Met on long-term loan from the Greek government.

As the single most comprehensive private collection of Cycladic art formed outside of Greece, the works include nearly all of the major types of marble figures and vessels from the Early Bronze Age, allowing viewers to explore and appreciate the essential aspects and subtle variations that Cycladic sculptors, with remarkable sensitivity, imbued in their sculptures over the course of more than a thousand years. The presentation offers an extraordinary opportunity to closely examine a large body of little known Cycladic works that have been repatriated to Greece.

The display of the Leonard N. Stern Collection of Cycladic Art at The Met is part of an innovative solution to repatriate the works to Greece while also bringing them to a worldwide audience and fostering international co-operation around the study and appreciation of Early Cycladic art and culture.
  
Exhibition Open: Ongoing

The Leonard N. Stern Collection on Loan from the Hellenic RepublicThe display of the Leonard N. Stern Collection of Cycladic Art at The Met is the result of a historic 50-year partnership among The Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Ministry of Culture of the Hellenic Republic, and the Museum of Cycladic Art in Athens—approved by Greek parliament in 2022. The partnership brings 161 splendid Cycladic works of art acquired by the businessman and philanthropist Leonard Stern over some 40 years to The Met on long-term loan from the Greek government.

As the single most comprehensive private collection of Cycladic art formed outside of Greece, the works include nearly all of the major types of marble figures and vessels from the Early Bronze Age, allowing viewers to explore and appreciate the essential aspects and subtle variations that Cycladic sculptors, with remarkable sensitivity, imbued in their sculptures over the course of more than a thousand years. The presentation offers an extraordinary opportunity to closely examine a large body of little known Cycladic works that have been repatriated to Greece.

The display of the Leonard N. Stern Collection of Cycladic Art at The Met is part of an innovative solution to repatriate the works to Greece while also bringing them to a worldwide audience and fostering international co-operation around the study and appreciation of Early Cycladic art and culture.
  
Exhibition Open: Ongoing

The Robert and Renée Belfer Court

On the east side of The Robert and Renée Belfer Court, this gallery is devoted to the Archaic period of about 700 to 480 B.C. The emphasis is on works that display the colorful, complex multiplicity that characterized the Greek world for more than two centuries.

Featured are the arts of the Greek colonies in western Asia Minor, Southern Italy, Sicily, Crete, and various areas of mainland Greece that flourished at this time: Corinth, Lakonia, Boeotia, Euboea, and Attica.

Mary and Michael Jaharis Gallery

The grand, barrel-vaulted Mary and Michael Jaharis Gallery displays large-scale sculpture and other monumental works of the sixth, fifth, and fourth centuries B.C.

Works of the sixth century include examples from the Museum's distinguished collection of Panathenaic amphorae amid other works related to ancient Greek athletics. In the center of the gallery are displayed large-scale marble copies—made during the Roman period—of bronze statues that were created in Greece during the fifth and fourth centuries but were lost or melted down over time. Original marble statues of the fourth century B.C. are shown by the crowning sculptures of tall Athenian grave monuments.

Judy and Michael H. Steinhardt Gallery

The Judy and Michael H. Steinhardt Gallery is unique in the Western Hemisphere in its display of the three major types of Greek freestanding marble sculpture of the sixth century B.C.: the kouros and kore, which served as funerary monuments or dedications, and the pillar-like grave stele. This is also the period when Athens supplanted Corinth as the center of pottery production, exemplified by several works attributed to the Amasis Painter, one of the most skillful and innovative of the black-figure artists.

The Bothmer Gallery I

The Bothmer Gallery I, named for Dietrich von Bothmer, former chairman of the Department of Greek and Roman Art, focuses on the art of Athens, particularly on painted vases in the black-figure technique, which flourished from about 600 to 530 B.C.

The vases were all made for actual use and they are decorated with mythological scenes as well as reflections of Athenian life. Contemporary works in marble, bronze, and silver present many of the same themes as on the pottery.

See also Gallery 157.

The Carolyn, Kate, Elizabeth, Thomas, and Jonathan Wiener Gallery 


The Carolyn, Kate, Elizabeth, Thomas, and Jonathan Wiener Gallery presents a fine collection of marble grave markers from Athens dating from the mid-fifth century B.C. through the early fourth century B.C.

In the fifth century B.C., the Greeks reached levels of excellence in art, literature, and philosophical thought that have served as models and formed the foundation for much of Western European culture. Despite wars—first against the Persians and then against Sparta, its major Greek rival—Athens became the political, economic, and cultural leader of Greece, producing works of art and literature of the highest quality.

The Bothmer Gallery II

The Bothmer Gallery II, named for Dietrich von Bothmer, provides examples of Athenian vase painting between about 530 and 400 B.C., when the flexibility of the newly developed red-figure technique permitted artists to draw freely over the convex and concave surfaces of vases.

A high proportion of the vases were made for containing, pouring, and drinking wine. On view also are significant examples of painted marble and terracotta sculpture, as well as coins and gems.

See also Gallery 155.

Spyros and Eurydice Costopoulos Gallery

Most of the works of art in the Spyros and Eurydice Costopoulos Gallery are funerary monuments, including large-scale marble vases that were used as grave markers.

In addition, terracotta statuettes of fashionable women or girls, prized today for their naturalness, vitality, and charm, are displayed here. The jewelry in this gallery presents the wealth of the Classical Greek world on the eve of the Hellenistic period that flourished after the death of Alexander the Great in 323 B.C.

Stavros and Danaë Costopoulos Gallery

The works of art displayed in the Stavros and Danaë Costopoulos Gallery reflect the artistic accomplishments of Athens at its height, as well as the influence that the Parthenon (built between 447 and 432 B.C.) had on objects in other media.

Prominent also are funerary vases covered with a white slip and decorated in a range of colors not previously used in Greek ceramics.

long_name.text: Greek Art: Fifth–Early Fourth Century B.C.

description.text:
For as long as humans have told stories, we’ve imagined creatures that transcend the natural world. Fantastical beings combining the features of animals, humans, and even plants appear across cultures, emerging in the most ancient myths and enduring in contemporary epics. The widespread presence of these supernatural beings, possessing the power to transform and be transformed, reflects a global impulse to make sense of both known and unknown worlds. Visual artists have given form to these imaginary creatures, resulting in some of the most fearsome, beloved, and extraordinary works of art ever made.

Set in the evocative atmosphere of The Met Cloisters, Creatures of Myth and Imagination: Europe and the Americas sheds light on a selection of works created on either side of the Atlantic Ocean between 500 and 1500 CE. The exhibition’s exploration of hybrid creatures deepens our understanding of their apparent necessity among diverse peoples. In the Americas, a complex gold pendant by a Tairona artist of northern Colombia, depicting a confrontational figure with hands on hips, a crocodile-like head, and an enormous headdress, would have reflected and expressed the wearer’s status and power. In Europe, ferocious dragons such as the one depicted on a monumental fresco from the monastery of San Pedro de Arlanza, Spain, took center stage to convey a multiplicity of meanings both sacred and profane.

With these and over 50 other objects, including sculpture, ceramics, ivories, textiles, paintings, and metalwork, Creatures of Myth and Imagination looks beyond a specific story, time, and place to explore larger questions about who we are and what connects us.
  
Exhibition Open: Through October 18, 2026

long_name.text: Creatures of Myth and Imagination: Europe and the Americas

description.text:
Leon Levy and Shelby White Court and Galleries
The Lamont Wing

The Leon Levy and Shelby White Gallery features Hellenistic portraits of marble and bronze, monumental painted terracotta vases from Southern Italy, and architectural fragments from the Temple of Artemis at Sardis, including the massive marble Ionic column and elements of the doorway, porch, and roof.

Sardis was the ancient capital of Lydia, in southwestern Turkey, and its temple to Artemis ranks among the seven largest of all Greek temples.

long_name.text: Hellenistic Sculpture and Architecture: Third–First Century B.C.

description.text:
The Lamont Wing

Greek emigrants founded colonies in Southern Italy and Sicily as early as the mid-eighth century B.C. They flourished in their own right and profoundly influenced the artistic production of the indigenous populations. This interplay is particularly evident in the South Italian vases of the fourth century B.C., produced especially for funerary use. Furthermore, it appears in the predilection for subjects derived from Greek drama. Owing to a dearth of fine marble, terracotta was also a primary medium for sculpture.

long_name.text: South Italian Art: Fourth–First Century B.C.

description.text:
Leon Levy and Shelby White Court and Galleries
The Lamont Wing

The spacious Leon Levy and Shelby White Court- a columned, rectangular peristyle with wide porticoes around the sides—evoke the splendor of imperial Rome. The natural light coming in through the roof beautifully highlights the plastic forms of the sculptures. Most works displayed here in central peristyle and the galleries opening to the left and the back belong to the period of the Roman Empire. In the middle of the Roman Court water splashes in a big stone basin, a modern pedestal fountain modeled on an imperial Roman counterpart. The original Roman gallery that opened in 1926 also had a fountain, set amid greenery to evoke the peristyle garden of a Roman villa. The current display inspires the same tranquility, although the atrium has a new polychrome floor of colored marble and the skylight has been raised to a second story which adds grandeur to the space as it was originally intended by the architects, McKim, Mead and White.

long_name.text: Leon Levy and Shelby White Court

description.text:
The Lamont Wing

Designed as an intimate space that is conducive to the appreciation of exquisite small-scale works of refined beauty, this gallery houses some of the most important treasures of Hellenistic art in the Museum's collection. It features gold, silver, and glass vessels from different regions of the Hellenistic world, including Magna Graecia, Egypt, the Ionian coast of Anatolia, and Parthia.

Fine necklaces, diadems, finger rings, earrings, and gold armbands provide a glimpse of the mastery of the goldsmiths' art for articles of personal adornment during the third through first centuries B.C.

Tarsi and John Georgas Family Gallery

The Lamont Wing

The Tarsi and John Georgas Family Gallery features a variety of aspects of Hellenistic art of the third through first centuries B.C. and their transmission to the Roman world. Hellenistic artists drew from the serene Classical ideal but also moved in new directions, reflecting the many contacts with various cultures after the conquests of Alexander the Great.

Wall paintings from a Roman villa at Boscoreale—some of which echo lost Hellenistic Macedonian royal paintings—and exquisite bronze sculptures provide an impression of the refined Classical environment that was cultivated, especially in private homes.

long_name.text: Hellenistic Art: Third Century B.C.–First Century A.D.

description.text:
The Lamont Wing

This is an accurate reconstruction of a bedroom from the Roman villa at Boscoreale (ca. 50–40 B.C.) that was buried by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius on August 24, A.D. 79. The walls are decorated with highly ornate and colorful frescoes of the so-called Second Style, comprising urban landscapes with towering architectural vistas on the side walls and rocky outdoor scenes populated by various songbirds on the rear wall.

Known as the villa of P. Fannius Synistor, the building was excavated in 1900.

Sylvia Josephs Berger and Joyce Berger Cowin Gallery

The Lamont Wing

The Sylvia Josephs Berger and Joyce Berger Cowin Gallery contains many exquisite examples of Roman imperial art. The reign of the first emperor Augustus (r. 27 B.C.–A.D. 14) saw the development of a new form of state art, the imperial portrait, created in both lifesize statuary and small-scale works such as gems and coins. Imperial patronage of the arts is evident in monumental architecture and sculpture as well as in other media such as wall paintings, silverware, and glass.

Much of the inspiration for the Augustan style came from the Greek East, as did many of the architects, sculptors, and artists themselves.

long_name.text: Art of Augustan Rome: Late First Century B.C.–First Century A.D.

description.text:
The Lamont Wing

This reconstruction of a room from the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase incorporates surviving panels from the original walls. They must have been painted by artists working for the imperial household during renovations after 11 B.C.

Compared to the paintings found at Boscoreale (see Gallery 165), these panels reflect a new and different style, known as Third Style, with a taste for insubstantial, delicate forms set against a flat, monochrome surface and including Egyptianizing motifs and medallions with portraits of members of the imperial family.

long_name.text: The Black Room: Late First Century B.C.

description.text:
The Lamont Wing

Gallery 168 displays fine examples of material culture from the second century A.D., when the Roman empire was at the height of its power and prosperity and when people in Rome, throughout Italy, and in many of the provinces enjoyed a standard of living and a way of life that were unequaled both in antiquity and in more recent times.

Objects associated with activities that either ensured or benefited from imperial protection (the Roman army, the state religion, the games, and the baths) are also on view.

long_name.text: Art of Imperial Rome: Second Century A.D.

description.text:
John A. and Carole O. Moran Gallery
The Lamont Wing

The works on view in the John A. and Carole O. Moran Gallery demonstrate changes in social and artistic trends that emerged during the later imperial period. One of these was the adoption of burial (internment) in place of cremation, prompting the creation of an empire-wide industry making large and imposing marble sarcophagi. Oriental cults spread across the Roman world and exerted a growing influence on people's lives in this period of political and economic uncertainty.

Also on view is a display of gold, silver, and bronze coins that were minted primarily to supply money for state expenditure and to facilitate the collection of taxes.

long_name.text: Art of the Later Roman Empire: Third Century A.D.

description.text: Seven world-famous tapestries depicting The Hunt of the Unicorn adorn this gallery. Against a landscape richly woven with lush flora and fauna, the mythical beast, possessed of magic powers, tries in vain to escape the men and dogs that pursue him. A large fireplace evokes the kind of aristocratic setting in which these tapestries, first recorded in Paris in 1680, originally would have been situated.

Leon Levy and Shelby White Court and Galleries
The Lamont Wing

The Etruscan heartland was delimited by the River Arno to the north and the Tiber to the south and east. Its culture developed from an earlier one, called Villanovan, and was enriched by a variety of influences, notably Phoenician, Greek, and Roman. By the early third century B.C., Rome effectively controlled most of the Etruscan territory.

The Etruscans were consummate metalworkers, as indicated by their jewelry and bronzes, most notably the ceremonial chariot from Monteleone. Another medium in which they excelled was carved amber. Their accomplished craftsmanship and cultural complexity is well illustrated in this gallery within the Leon Levy and Shelby White Galleries.

The Leonard N. Stern Collection on Loan from the Hellenic RepublicThe display of the Leonard N. Stern Collection of Cycladic Art at The Met is the result of a historic 50-year partnership among The Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Ministry of Culture of the Hellenic Republic, and the Museum of Cycladic Art in Athens—approved by Greek parliament in 2022. The partnership brings 161 splendid Cycladic works of art acquired by the businessman and philanthropist Leonard Stern over some 40 years to The Met on long-term loan from the Greek government.

As the single most comprehensive private collection of Cycladic art formed outside of Greece, the works include nearly all of the major types of marble figures and vessels from the Early Bronze Age, allowing viewers to explore and appreciate the essential aspects and subtle variations that Cycladic sculptors, with remarkable sensitivity, imbued in their sculptures over the course of more than a thousand years. The presentation offers an extraordinary opportunity to closely examine a large body of little known Cycladic works that have been repatriated to Greece.

The display of the Leonard N. Stern Collection of Cycladic Art at The Met is part of an innovative solution to repatriate the works to Greece while also bringing them to a worldwide audience and fostering international co-operation around the study and appreciation of Early Cycladic art and culture.
  
Exhibition Open: Ongoing
This gallery features a rotating display of special exhibitions organized by the curators of the Department of Greek and Roman Art.

The Norbert Schimmel Gallery
The Lamont Wing

The Norbert Schimmel Gallery houses a collection of ancient art from Cyprus that was acquired by the Museum in 1874, having been assembled on the island by General Luigi Palma di Cesnola, who was American consul there and later became the Met's first director. Because of the sites that he explored, he did not find the very earliest levels of material culture, which are now dated back to 10,000 B.C.

This gallery focuses on objects of the Bronze Age (ca. 2500–1050 B.C.). Though not surely from Cyprus, the bronze ingot represents the island's single most significant resource.

long_name.text: Prehistoric Art from Cyprus: Seventh Millennium–ca. 1000 B.C.

description.text:
The greatest strength of the Museum's Cypriot collection lies in stone sculpture of the sixth through third century B.C., examples of which are on view in this gallery. Made of the soft local limestone, the works preserve considerable polychromy.

The most significant object in terms of scale, quality, and importance is the sarcophagus from the site of Amathus. Also well represented here are metal vases, particularly of silver. The works on display reflect the successive influence of Assyrian, Egyptian, Persian, and Greek domination.

long_name.text: Archaic Art from Cyprus: ca. 1000–ca. 480 B.C.

description.text:
A.G. Leventis Foundation Gallery

The Lamont Wing

Although Cyprus was under Persian rule from about 525 B.C. until it was liberated by Alexander the Great in 333 B.C., the Cypriot sculpture on view in the A.G. Leventis Foundation Gallery demonstrates Greek influence, though in a form modified by the variety of peoples that occupied the island.

For this period especially, sculpture represents the great strength of the Museum's holdings. The sarcophagus from Golgoi is the most important work. The lively scenes decorating it would have been enhanced with added color, traces of which remain on many of the other pieces in the Cypriot galleries.

long_name.text: Classical Art from Cyprus: ca. 480–ca. 310 B.C.

description.text:
Jake and Nancy Hamon Gallery

This gallery features works of Cypriot art from the periods of Hellenistic and Roman rule. Artistic production followed the prevailing styles of the Hellenistic world, although regional traditions continued—as represented, for example, by the sculptures of "temple boys." The art of Roman Cyprus is particularly well illustrated by glass and jewelry as well as sculpture.

long_name.text: The Jake and Nancy Hamon Gallery

description.text:
A series of tapestries devoted to Nine Heroes of the Middle Ages celebrated in a fourteenth-century poem dominate the room. From the original ensemble, five survive: David and Joshua of Hebrew scripture; Hector and Julius Caesar from Classical tradition; and Arthur, the legendary king of England.

Thought once to have belonged to Jean of France, Duke of Berry, these are among the earliest tapestries to survive from the Middle Ages.

The room features the famed Early Netherlandish masterpiece the Annunciation Triptych, also known as the Merode Altarpiece, after its previous owner. The wings—including the donor and his wife and Joseph in his workshop—surround the central panel of the Annunciation.

Reflecting the function of this altarpiece, many of the other works of art in the room were intended as aids to private devotion, including a miniature carved and painted altarpiece and a mother-of-pearl triptych. The decorative feature is a fifteenth-century Spanish painted ceiling.

name.text: 19th and Early 20th Century European Paintings and Sculpture

description.text: The Met's world-famed collection of European paintings encompasses works of art from the 13th through the 19th centuries—from Giotto to Gauguin. Most, though not all, are displayed in the galleries of the Department of European Paintings. Others works of art can be found in the Lehman Collection, the Linsky Collection, The Met Cloisters, and in various period rooms. Apart from its many individual masterpieces by artists as diverse as Jan van Eyck, Caravaggio, and Seurat, the Museum possesses the most extensive collection of 17th-century Dutch art in the western hemisphere, including outstanding works by Frans Hals, Rembrandt, and Vermeer. Its holdings of El Greco and Goya are the finest outside of Spain, while the survey it offers of French painting between neo-Classicism and post-Impressionism is second only to Paris, with extensive holdings of the work of Corot, Courbet, Manet, Monet, Degas, Cézanne, and Van Gogh.

long_name.text: 19th and Early 20th Century European Paintings and Sculpture

description.text:
More than three thousand limestone blocks, lent to The Cloisters by the Spanish government in 1957, constitute the twelfth-century apse that dominates this gallery. The interior of the half dome is decorated with a Catalan fresco depicting the Virgin and Child in Majesty and the Adoration of the Magi from the church of the Virgin near Tredòs, and a magnificent twelfth-century painted Spanish wood crucifix hangs from the arch. Also displayed in the gallery is a twelfth-century Italian doorway from Tuscany, as well as other works from the Romanesque period.

The documentary The Fuentidueña Apse: A Journey from Castile to New York, produced and directed by Christopher Noey, chronicles the dismantling of the twelfth-century apse from the church of San Martín in Fuentidueña, Spain, and its reconstruction at The Cloisters, between 1958 and 1961.

The largest of the galleries at The Cloisters, the Late Gothic Hall houses German, Spanish, and Italian paintings and sculpture of the later fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries, most of which came from large dismantled altarpieces.

The many outstanding works include the Three Kings from the abbey at Lichtenthal, near Baden-Baden, two sculptures by Tilman Riemenschneider, and the 1434 marble altarpiece by Andrea da Giona. Under the four flamboyant Gothic windows from Soissons is a large tapestry hanging representing "Christ is Born as Man's Redeemer."

The Great Hall Balcony (Gallery 200 through Gallery 205) traces the development of Chinese ceramics, including the creation of vibrant green and blue (or celadon) glazes of the tenth to the thirteenth century and the flowering of porcelains painted in underglaze cobalt blue in the fourteenth. Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

The Great Hall Balcony (Gallery 200 through Gallery 205) traces the development of Chinese ceramics, including the creation of vibrant green and blue (or celadon) glazes of the tenth to the thirteenth century and the flowering of porcelains painted in underglaze cobalt blue in the fourteenth. Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

Adele and Stanley Herzman Collection

The Great Hall Balcony (Gallery 200 through Gallery 205) traces the development of Chinese ceramics, including the creation of vibrant green and blue (or celadon) glazes of the tenth to the thirteenth century and the flowering of porcelains painted in underglaze cobalt blue in the fourteenth. Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

The Arthur M. Sackler Gallery
Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Arthur M. Sackler Gallery houses part of the Museum's holdings of Chinese Buddhist sculpture, the largest collection of its kind in the West. (Additional examples are featured in Gallery 208.) Elegant bronze icons, monumental stone sculptures, and colorful sculptures made of wood, clay, and lacquer illustrate China's complicated adaptation of the Indian religion of Buddhism. Early Chinese Buddhist sculpture often shows ties to Indian and Central Asian traditions; after the twelfth century, however, when Buddhism disappeared in India, Chinese sculptures often illustrate exchanges with artistic traditions from Nepal and Tibet.

Highlights of the collection include several monumental stone sculptures, a nearly lifesize gilt-bronze sculpture of Buddha Maitreya dated 486, an example of a seventh-century piece made using the dry lacquer technique, and rare and important wood sculptures with inscriptions dating to 1282 and 1385.

The traditional East Asian lunar calendar marks time through a twelve-year cycle, each year represented by the animals of the Chinese zodiac: rat, ox, tiger, rabbit, dragon, snake, horse, ram, monkey, rooster, dog, and pig. First associated with the Chinese calendar in the third century BCE and firmly established by the first century CE, these symbolic creatures are believed to embody certain traits that are manifested in the personalities of people born in that animal’s year. February 17, 2026, marks the beginning of the Year of the Horse, a creature symbolizing power, strength, and vitality.



Celebrating the Year of the Horse brings together works from The Met collection to explore the horse’s enduring nature and vital place in Chinese civilization. Domesticated in prehistoric times, this animal has shaped nearly every facet of life—from agriculture and transportation to trade, warfare, sport, and entertainment. In ancient China, horse-drawn chariots transported military commanders and the social elite, while in the Tang dynasty (618–907), tall and robust horses came to embody the strength of the state and cosmopolitan spirit of the empire. In Chinese popular culture, horses are among the most celebrated animals, and in the spiritual realm they serve as noble mounts for divine guardians believed to bring joy, protection, and prosperity to the household. This presentation, featuring a range of expressive works in ceramics, glass, jade, and metal, and woodblock prints, illuminates these roles while celebrating the horse’s power, vitality, and spirit.
  
Exhibition Open: Through January 26, 2027

long_name.text: Celebrating the Year of the Horse

description.text:
Charlotte C. Weber Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The largest in the West, the Museum's collection of Chinese Buddhist sculpture is featured in Gallery 206 and Gallery 208. Elegant bronze icons, monumental stone sculptures, and colorful sculptures made of wood, clay, and lacquer illustrate China's complicated adaptation of the Indian religion of Buddhism. Early Chinese Buddhist sculpture often shows ties to Indian and Central Asian traditions; after the twelfth century, however, when Buddhism disappeared in India, Chinese sculptures often illustrate exchanges with artistic traditions from Nepal and Tibet.

Highlights of the collection include several monumental stone sculptures, a nearly lifesize gilt-bronze sculpture of Buddha Maitreya dated 486, an example of a seventh-century piece made using the dry lacquer technique, and rare and important wood sculptures with inscriptions dating to 1282 and 1385.

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The most distinctive feature of the Astor forecourt is the round "moon gate" that forms the entrance to The Court and its adjoining reception room displaying Ming dynasty hardwood furniture. Located at the heart of the Asian Art galleries, the forecourt is a crossroads that links the galleries for the arts of China, Japan, and South and Southeast Asia.

Douglas Dillon Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The galleries for Chinese painting and calligraphy are comprised of seven separate rooms (Gallery 210 through Gallery 216), including the Douglas Dillon Galleries, the C. C. Wang Family Gallery, and the Frances Young Tang Gallery. Together these rooms constitute the largest permanent space for the display of Chinese painting and related arts outside Asia and house one of the world's finest collections of its kind, with works ranging in date from the eighth century to the present.

Due to the light sensitivity of works on silk and paper, displays are regularly rotated to feature either special loan exhibitions or thematic installations that highlight different aspects of the collection.

C.C. Wang Family Gallery

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

Wang Family Gallery, and the Frances Young Tang Gallery. Together these rooms constitute the largest permanent space for the display of Chinese painting and related arts outside Asia and house one of the world's finest collections of its kind, with works ranging in date from the eighth century to the present.

Due to the light sensitivity of works on silk and paper, displays are regularly rotated to feature either special loan exhibitions or thematic installations that highlight different aspects of the collection.

The galleries for Chinese painting and calligraphy are comprised of seven separate rooms (Gallery 210 through Gallery 216), including the Douglas Dillon Galleries, the C. C. Wang Family Gallery, and the Frances Young Tang Gallery. Together these rooms constitute the largest permanent space for the display of Chinese painting and related arts outside Asia and house one of the world's finest collections of its kind, with works ranging in date from the eighth century to the present.

Due to the light sensitivity of works on silk and paper, displays are regularly rotated to feature either special loan exhibitions or thematic installations that highlight different aspects of the collection.

The Astor Court

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

Inspired by Brooke Russell Astor, who spent part of her childhood in China, The Astor Court and its adjoining reception room (Gallery 218) featuring Ming dynasty hardwood furniture opened to the public in 1981, a gift of the Vincent Astor Foundation.

Modeled on a seventeenth-century courtyard in the Garden of the Master of the Fishing Nets in Suzhou, the court was entirely constructed using traditional tools and techniques. An eighteenth-century imperial kiln was reopened to fire the ceramic tiles; rare nan wood was hand-planed into columns; specimen Taihu rocks were used for the rockeries, and a granite terrace was hand chiseled from a Suzhou quarry.

A team of twenty-six Chinese craftsmen (along with a chef) spent six months in New York assembling the components.

long_name.text: Chinese Courtyard in the Style of the Ming Dynasty

description.text:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries for Chinese Decorative Arts

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

Opened on May 19, 2014, the Chinese Treasury re-creates the type of collecting and display found in the treasure cabinets (duo bao ge) assembled throughout the late Ming (1368–1644) and Qing (1644–1911) dynasties. Extraordinary works in ivory, rhinoceros horn, glass, porcelain, and jade and other hard stones celebrate the skill and imagination of Chinese artists in designing and crafting these materials. Touchpads allow viewers to read introductory texts for all of the objects and to explore further by grouping the works of art by material and by themes.

Enamel decoration is a significant element of Chinese decorative arts that has long been overlooked. This exhibition reveals the aesthetic, technical, and cultural achievement of Chinese enamel wares by demonstrating the transformative role of enamel during the Ming (1368–1644) and Qing (1644–1911) dynasties. The first transformational moment occurred in the late 14th to 15th century, when the introduction of cloisonné enamel from the West, along with the development of porcelain with overglaze enamels, led to a shift away from a monochromatic palette to colorful works. The second transformation occurred in the late 17th to 18th century, when European enameling materials and techniques were brought to the Qing court and more subtle and varied color tones were developed on enamels applied over porcelain, metal, glass, and other mediums. In both moments, Chinese artists did not simply adopt or copy foreign techniques; they actively created new colors and styles that reflected their own taste. The more than 100 objects on view are drawn mainly from The Met collection.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:
Rotation 1: July 4, 2022–April 30, 2023
Rotation 2: May 20, 2023–March 24, 2024 
Rotation 3: April 13, 2024–February 16, 2025
Rotation 4: March 1, 2025–January 20, 2026
Rotation 5: January 22–June 28, 2026



This exhibition is made possible by the Florence and Herbert Irving Fund for Asian Art Exhibitions.

long_name.text: Embracing Color: Enamel in Chinese Decorative Arts, 1300–1900

description.text:
More than a hundred remarkable objects from the Heber Bishop collection, including carvings of jade, the most esteemed stone in China, and many other hardstones, are on view in this focused presentation. The refined works represent the sophisticated art of Chinese gemstone carvers during the Qing dynasty (1644–1911) as well as the highly accomplished skills of Mogul Indian (1526–1857) craftsmen, which provided an exotic inspiration to their Chinese counterparts. Also on view are a set of Chinese stone-working tools and illustrations of jade workshops, which will introduce the traditional method of working jade.

The exhibition is made possible by the Florence and Herbert Irving Fund for Asian Art Exhibitions.
  
Exhibition Open: Through June 28, 2026

long_name.text: A Passion for Jade: The Bishop Collection

description.text:
From the earliest hand-built figurines rooted in spiritual beliefs to the vibrant works that define today’s contemporary ceramic art scene, Japanese pottery reflects exceptional creativity and a refined sense of beauty. Its distinctiveness arises from the ingenuity and mastery of the potters, as well as the wide range of wares that have long supported daily life. This exhibition explores Japan’s extensive and rich history of ceramic art through approximately 350 extraordinary works presented in themes that offer fresh perspectives on the diverse forms and functions, from everyday tableware to vessels created for tea masters and elite households to modern sculptural compositions.

Japan’s earliest pottery dates back more than 12,000 years, when makers shaped and fired clay vessels for cooking and storage—objects that bear the traces of these early potters’ hands. Expertise transmitted from China and Korea in medieval times facilitated the development of new Japanese techniques and wares. Over time, the reverence for prized ceramics led to finding beauty even through damage. Japanese artisans developed kintsugi, the art of repairing broken ceramics with gold lacquer, restoring the damaged object’s function while making it even more precious and valuable. Japanese cuisine and dining aesthetics inspired vessels designed to harmonize with the color and texture of food. Many porcelains from the Edo period (1615–1868) are embellished with motifs drawn from the natural world or symbols of happiness, longevity, and good fortune.

Featuring refined ceramics and other artworks from the Museum’s Harry G. C. Packard Collection, this exhibition celebrates the 50th anniversary of this landmark acquisition that established the foundation of The Met’s Japanese art holdings. By placing ceramics in dialogue with related art forms—including lacquers, textiles, paintings, and woodblock prints—the display inspires curiosity about the multiverse of Japanese ceramics and its broader cultural contexts.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:
Rotation 1: January 19–May 12, 2026
Rotation 2: May 14–August 16, 2026
Rotation 3: August 29–December 8, 2026
Rotation 4: December 10, 2026–March 28, 2027
Rotation 5: April 10–August 8, 2027
  
Exhibition Open: Through August 8, 2027

long_name.text: The Infinite Artistry of Japanese Ceramics

description.text:
Most objects are displayed to show their “best” angle meant to impress, sometimes hiding intriguing details. Flip Sides: Seeing Korean Art Anew invites close looking and offers multiple views of the inside, reverse, or hard-to-see aspects of objects.



Bringing together approximately 50 objects from The Met collection, with more than half displayed for the first time, this exhibition, with a rotation, shows treasured Korean traditions. By presenting them in ways that give us a fuller picture into each object’s unique form, Flip Sides provides insight into the construction and function of works in metal, wood, ceramic, textiles, and lacquer. A Buddhist sculpture that held offerings inside, a porcelain jar of striking openwork that conceals an inner chamber, a bronze mirror with a delicately incised image, and a king’s lacquer letter box with linings featuring impressive calligraphy are among the featured objects in Flip Sides.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:
Rotation 1: March 16–October 18, 2026
Rotation 2: October 31, 2026–May 31, 2027
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 31, 2027

long_name.text: Flip Sides: Seeing Korean Art Anew

description.text:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving South Asian Galleries (Gallery 234 through Gallery 243) represent India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. They primarily trace the development of the sculptural arts associated with the temples and shrines of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. The arts of the Indian subcontinent are represented from early centuries B.C. up to around the sixteenth century, and encompass all the major regional and dynastic styles. Highlight areas include early arts of Shunga India, Buddhist art of the Gandharan and Amaravati regions, the Kushan and Gupta dynasties of northern India, the Kashmiri kingdoms, Pala eastern India, Pandyan and Chola south India, and medieval northern India.

Note: Islamic arts of South Asia are displayed in the galleries overseen by the Department of Islamic Art.

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

description.text:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving Southeast Asian galleries (Gallery 244 through Gallery 250) are devoted to the early bronze cultures, spanning from the early centuries B.C. to the first millenium A.D. and to the sculpture of the Hindu-Buddhist period, eighth to eighteenth century. The latter encompass the arts of Burma (Myanmar), Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Indonesia. Major strengths include pre–Angkorian Khmer and related stone and bronze sculpture, Javanese gold jewelry and bronze sculpture, and glazed ceramics from Thailand and Vietnam.

Note: The arts of the non–Hindu-Buddhist cultures from Indonesia and the Philippines are exhibited in Gallery 355, within the galleries for the Arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas.

In Hinduism, the act of darshan, or “seeing god,” is central to worship. This intimate exchange between deity and devotee traditionally takes place in the temple. But within each home is also a shrine, dedicated to that householder’s chosen deity. The need to display an image of the divine in the home was traditionally fulfilled by small icons made of clay or metal. In the mid-19th century, new technologies were introduced into India, first photography and then the chromolithographic press. The latter permitted the production of inexpensive prints of the Hindu gods for mass consumption. These proved immensely popular and for the first time in India, even the humblest home could afford a colorful icon of their chosen god to display in the household shrine.

Household Gods: Hindu Devotional Prints, 1860–1930 presents the first encyclopedic exhibition of these chromolithographic prints from the pioneering studio presses of Calcutta (Kolkata), Poona (Pune), and Bombay (Mumbai). These mass-produced prints became a powerful means of expressing Indian religious identity at a time when the country was experiencing the first stirrings of the Independence movement.

Featuring approximately 120 works, shown in four rotations, from The Met’s collection of chromolithographic prints, along with paintings and portable triptych shrines, Household Gods provides a unique window on the vibrant tradition of Indian devotional imagery on the cusp of modernity.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:
Rotation 1: January 24–May 25, 2026
Rotation 2: May 30–October 11, 2026
Rotation 3: October 17, 2026–February 21, 2027
Rotation 4: February 27–June 27, 2027
  
Exhibition Open: Through June 27, 2027

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The religious arts of Tibet and Nepal are represented in Gallery 252 and Gallery 253 by important early cloth paintings (tangkas), including rare portrait paintings, and by Vajrayana Buddhist sculptures. The early Nepalese metal, stone, and wood sculptures form a major ensemble, as do the Buddhist and Hindu painted book covers. Ritual utensils such as crowns, weapons, and libation implements form another important aspect of the displays. The tangkas and manuscript objects are rotated periodically.

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing
